Role Of Parser :
•Role of parser consist Syntax Analyzer
•Syntax Analyzer creates the syntactic structure of the given source
program.
•This syntactic structure is mostly a parse tree.
•Syntax Analyzer is also known as parser.
•The syntax of a programming is described by a context -free grammar
(CFG). We will use BNF (Backus Naur Form) notation in the description of
CFGs.
•The syntax analyzer (parser) checks whether a given source program
satisfies the rules implied by a context -free grammar or not.

• Wecategorize theparsers into two groups:
1. Top-Down Parser
–the parse tree is created top to bottom, starting from the root.
2. Bottom -UpParser
–the parse is created bottom to top; starting from the leaves
•Both top -down and bottom -up parsers scan the input from left to
right (one symbol at a time).
•Efficient top -down and bottom -up parsers can be implemented only
for sub -classes of context -free grammars.
–LL for top -down parsing
–LR for bottom -up parsing

Techniques for writing grammars for programming languages
(removal left recursion, left Factor )
A context -free grammar is said to be left recursive if it contains a production rule where
the non -terminal on the left -hand side of the rule also appears as the first symbol on the
right -hand side.
In other words, the grammar is trying to define a non -terminal in terms of itself, creating
a recursive loop.
This can be represented formally as −
A→A α|β
Where −
Ais a non -terminal symbol.
αrepresents a sequence of terminals and/or non -terminals.
βrepresents another sequence of terminals and/or non -terminals.

[Page 5]
Left Factoring
After the left recursion, let us see the idea of left factoring.
While left recursion presents a parsing challenge, left factoring is a desirable property
for top -down parsing.
It involves restructuring the grammar to eliminate common prefixes in production rules.
Importance of left factoring:
When a grammar has multiple production rules for a non -terminal with a common
prefix, the parser faces ambiguity during the parsing process.
It has to choose between these rules without knowing which one will ultimately lead to
a successful parse.
Left factoring helps in resolving this ambiguity by postponing the decision point, making
the parsing process more efficient.

Performing Left Factoring
The process of left factoring involves identifying the longest common prefix (α) in the
alternatives for a non -terminal and rewriting the rules to factor out this common prefix.
Consider a grammar with the following rules −
A→αβ1|αβ2|…|αβ n|γ
Here, 'α' is the longest common prefix in the first 'n' alternatives for non -terminal 'A’.
We can left factor this as follows −
A→α A′|γ
A→β1|β2|…|βn

Non Context Free language Constructs
There are some language constructions in the programming languages which are not
context -free.
This means that, we cannot write a context -free grammar for these constructions.
• L1 = { ωcω | ω is in ( a|b)*} is not context -free Grammer declaring an identifier and
checking whether it is declared or not later.
We cannot do this with a context -free language. We need semantic analyzer (which is not
context -free).
• L2 = { anb mc ndm | n≥1 and m≥1 } is not context -free 🡺 declaring two functions (one
with n parameters, the other one with m parameters), and then calling them with actual
parameters .

•These attributes may provide information about the storage allocated for a name, its type ,
its scope (where in the program its value may be used) in the case of procedure -names,
such things as the number and types of its arguments, the method of passing each
argument (for example, by value or by reference), and the type returned .Symbol tables

[Page 5]
It is used by various phases of compiler as follows :-
•Lexical Analysis : Creates new table entries in the table, example like entries about token .
•Syntax Analysis : Adds information regarding attribute type, scope, dimension, line of
reference, use, etc in the table .
•Semantic Analysis : Uses available information in the table to check for semantics i.e. to
verify that expressions and assignments are semantically correct(type checking) and update
it accordingly .
•Intermediate Code generation : Refers symbol table for knowing how much and what type
of run-time is allocated and table helps in adding temporary variable information .
•Code Optimization : Uses information present in symbol table for machine dependent
optimization .
•Target Code generation : Generates code by using address information of identifier present
in the table .

•In this method, an array is used to store names and associated information .
•A pointer “available” is maintained at end of all stored records and new names are added

•To search for a name we start from beginning of list till available pointer and if not found
we get an error “use of undeclared name”
•While inserting a new name we must ensure that it is not already present otherwise error
occurs i.e. “Multiple defined name”
•Insertion is fast O(1), but lookup is slow for large tables –O(n) on average

2. Linked List
•This implementation is using linked list. A link field is added to each record .
•Searching of names is done in order pointed by link of link field .
•A pointer “First” is maintained to point to first record of symbol table .
•Insertion is fast O(1), but lookup is slow for large tables –O(n) on average .
3. Hash Table
•In hashing scheme two tables are maintained –a hash table and symbol table and is the most
commonly used method to implement symbol tables .
•A hash table is an array with index range : 0 to table size–1.
These entries are pointer pointing to names of symbol table .
•To search for a name we use hash function that will result in any integer between 0 to table
size–1.
•Insertion and lookup can be made very fast– O(1).
•Advantage is quick search is possible and disadvantage is that hashing is complicated to
implement .

•In the source program, every name possesses a region of validity, called the scope of that
name .
•A compiler maintains two types of symbol tables : a global symbol table which can be
accessed by all the procedures and scope symbol tables that are created for each scope in the
program .
•To determine the scope of a name, symbol tables are arranged in hierarchical structure .
•The rules in a block -structured language are as follows :
•If a name declared within block B then it will be valid only within B.
•If B1 block is nested within B2 then the name that is valid for block B2 is also valid for B1
unless the name's identifier is re-declared in B1.Representation of “scope”

•Tables are organized into stack and each table contains the list of names and their
associated attributes.
•Whenever a new block is entered then a new table is entered onto the stack. The new table
holds the name that is declared as local to this block.
•When the declaration is compiled then the table is searched for a name.
•If the name is not found in the table then the new name is inserted.
•When the name's reference is translated then each table is searched, starting from the each
table on the stack.Representation of “scope” Continue…

SDD for Control flow statements
•Si is formed from B.code and Si.code .
•We use a local variable begin to hold a new label attached to
the first instruction for this while -statement, which is also the
first instruction for B.
•We use a variable rather than an attribute, because begin is
local to the semantic rules for this production .
•The inherited label S.next marks the instruction that control
must flow to if B is false ; hence, B.false is set to be S.next . A new
label B.true is attached to the first instruction for Si; the code for

[Page 5]
Semantic Error Recovery
•The purpose of recovery is to report the error and continue compilation
without stopping. This prevents multiple cascading errors.
•Recovery from Undeclared Identifier
– If the compiler encounters a variable that has not been declared :
x = y + z; // y undeclared
Recovery Action:
The compiler creates a new symbol table entry for y, usually assigning it a
default type (int) .
This allows compilation to continue.

Procedures?
Task -specific:

Monitoring and Evaluation:

performance of tasks.
•By following documented procedures, organizations can assess
efficiency, identify bottlenecks, and improve processes over time.
Audit and Accountability:
•Procedures contribute to the accountability of individuals and the
organization as a whole.
•In the event of audits, procedures provide a basis for assessing
compliance and identifying areas for improvement.

needs of record keeping of an organization.

Billing Systems
Purchasing System

yearly reports or any on demand reports depending upon the needs of
organization. Transaction Process System deals with routine transactions
effectively.

[Page 38]
What is Data and Information?
•Data is a collection of raw, unorganized facts and details like text,
observations, figures, symbols and descriptions of things etc.
•In other words, data does not carry any specific purpose and has no
significance by itself. Moreover, data is measured in terms of bits and bytes –
which are basic units of information in the context of computer storage and
processing.
•Information is processed, organized and structured data. It provides context
for data and enables decision making. For example, a single customer’s sale at
a restaurant is data –this becomes information when the business is able to
identify the most popular or least popular dish.

[Page 42]
Need for Information security
▪Confidentiality:
Protecting the confidentiality of sensitive information is paramount. Information
security measures ensure that only authorized individuals or systems have access
to confidential data, preventing unauthorized disclosure.
▪Integrity:
Information integrity ensures that data remains accurate, consistent, and unaltered.
Security measures help prevent unauthorized modifications or tampering,
maintaining the reliability of information.
▪Availability:
Information security safeguards are crucial to ensuring that information and
information systems are available when needed. This includes protecting against
disruptions, such as cyberattacks , natural disasters, or technical failures.

[Page 50]
Goal of information assurance?
The purpose of IA is to reduce information risks by ensuring the information on which the
business makes decisions is reliable. This purpose is achieved by following:
Risk management: Businesses face legal fines and penalties if the information in the network
is compromised. IA enables risk assessment to identify vulnerabilities and the potential impact
on the business in terms of compliance, cost and operational continuity. The goal is to
mitigate potential threats.
Encryption at rest and in transit: IA mandates end -to-endencryption to protect privacy by
ensuring no human or computer can read data at rest and in transit except the intended
parties. The goal is to help businesses stay compliant with regulatory requirements and
standards.
Data integrity: Bad business decisions usually stem from bad data. IA focuses on auditing
data collection and tracking process, improving transparency in the organizational process.
The goal is to manage data in a way that a future audit can retrace the process, leading to
better decision -making.

[Page 56]
Cyber security risk analysis?
❑Asset Identification:
Identify and catalog all digital assets within the organization, including hardware, software, data,
networks, and personnel.
❑Threat Identification:
Identify potential threats and vulnerabilities that could exploit weaknesses in the organization's
cybersecurity posture. This includes external threats (e.g., hackers, malware) and internal threats (e.g.,
insider threats).
❑Vulnerability Assessment:
Conduct a systematic evaluation of the organization's systems and networks to identify vulnerabilities.
This may involve using automated tools, penetration testing, and other techniques.
❑Likelihood Assessment:
Assess the likelihood of each identified threat exploiting vulnerabilities. Consider factors such as
historical data, threat intelligence, and the organization's security controls.

[Page 57]
Cybersecurity risk analysis?
❑Documentation and Reporting:
Document the entire risk analysis process, including the identified risks, assessments,
mitigation strategies, and residual risks. Provide clear and concise reports for stakeholders.
❑Continuous Monitoring and Review:
Regularly monitor the cybersecurity landscape, update risk assessments as needed, and
adapt mitigation strategies to address emerging threats and changes in the organization's IT
environment.

[Page 58]
Cybersecurity risk analysis?
❑Impact Assessment:
Evaluate the potential impact of a cybersecurity incident on the organization's assets, operations,
reputation, and financial standing. Consider both tangible and intangible consequences.
❑Risk Calculation:
Calculate the overall risk for each identified threat by combining the likelihood and impact
assessments. This may involve assigning numerical values or qualitative ratings to different risk levels.
❑Risk Prioritization:
Prioritize risks based on their calculated risk levels. This helps organizations focus their resources on
addressing the most significant and potentially damaging threats.
❑Mitigation Strategies:
Develop and implement strategies to mitigate or reduce identified risks. This may involve
implementing security controls, improving security awareness, updating software, or enhancing
incident response capabilities.

communication.

It enables devices to send and receive data.
Every device —whether it’s a smartphone, computer, or printer —has an IP address.

IP ADDRESS
Public & Private IP Address
Public Used to connect to the internet. Globally unique.
Private Used within local networks. Not accessible globally.
Private IP Ranges (IPv4):

IP ADDRESS
Static IP
•A fixed IP address that does not change . It is manually assigned to a device
and stays the same even after restarting.
•A company runs a website at 203.0.113.10. Anyone typing that IP or domain (linked to
that IP) reaches their server —the IP never changes.
Dynamic IP
•An IP address that is automatically assigned by a DHCP(Dynamic Host
Configuration Protocol) server and may change over time .
•You connect your laptop to Wi -Fi. Today, your IP is 192.168.1.15, but tomorrow it
might be 192.168.1.20 —assigned automatically by the router (DHCP).

[Page 21]
Criteria for measuring Severity of Vulnerability
1.True Positive (TP): Correct Alert
The system correctly detects a real threat.
Example: You scan a vulnerable system and the scanner detects the actual
vulnerability.
2.False Positive (FP): Wrong Alert
The system raises an alert, but there's no actual threat.
Example: The scanner says a port is vulnerable, but it’s actually safe
and patched .

Criteria for measuring Severity of Vulnerability
3. True Negative (TP): Correct Silence
The system correctly identifies that there's no threat.
Example: A system is safe, and the scanner does not raise an alert.
4.False Neg ative (FP): Missed Threat
The system fails to detect a real threat.
Example: A system has a known vulnerability, but the scanner misses
it and says it's clean.

- manipulate (like files, database records) without
- resources user accounts, or proper
- authorization, just by changing a reference (ID) in the request.
- https://bank.com/user/profile?user_id=1234
- If an attacker changes 1234 to 1235 and gets access to another user's profile
- without permission, that's IDOR.
- an
- Prevention: Implement proper access controls, validate user input, and use
- indirect references rather than direct references

PROBE
A probe is a tool or method used to gather information about a target system,
network, or device —often without causing harm or noticeable changes . Probes
are used for:
•Monitoring
•Testing
•Vulnerability assessment
•Intrusion detection
•Reconnaissance (in ethical hacking or attacks)
Think of a probe like gently knocking on different doors of a building to find out
which ones are open, without breaking anything.
Like Ping ( simply to see whether the destination actually exists)

Types of Probe
Vulnerability Probe :
A Vulnerability Probe goes beyond just asking for info —it sends special
inputs (called payloads ) to test if the system has a real security flaw .
Key Points:
Confirms real vulnerabilities by actively testing the system .
May carry some risk —it could:
•Crash the application
•Expose sensitive data
•Cause denial of service
But they’re more accurate than just guessing from ports or banners.(poking)
{A payload is the action part of a cyberattack —what actually does the
damage or testing after the attack reaches the target.}

2.ETTERCAP
What Ettercap Can Do:
•Can intercept and alter traffic between two devices.
•Captures passwords and eavesdrops on protocols like HTTP, FTP, Telnet, etc.
•Uses techniques like ARP poisoning to become a “man in the middle.”
Modes of Operation in Ettercap:
1.IP-based –Filters packets using IP addresses.
2.MAC -based –Filters based on device MAC addresses (helpful for sniffing
through gateways).
3.ARP -based –Poisons ARP tables to capture traffic between two devices.

Network Defense tools
Network defense tools play a crucial role in protecting computer networks from various cyber
threats and attacks. These tools help organizations monitor, detect, and respond to security
incidents, ensuring the integrity, confidentiality, and availability of their network resources.
Example:
•Antivirus and Anti -malware Software (McAfee, Windows Defender).
•Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) and Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS) (Snort,
Suricata , Cisco Firepower)
•Firewalls (Cisco ASA, Palo Alto Networks, pfSense ).
•Virtual Private Network (VPN) ( OpenVPN , Cisco AnyConnect, Palo Alto
GlobalProtect ).

- through a network. It lets multiple devices in a private network share a

•Multi -Layer Inspection:
Layer 3/4 (Basic Identity/Purpose): They check your ID (IP address) and your stated
purpose (port/protocol ).
Layer 7 (Content/Behavior): If you try to leave with a large, suspicious bag that wasn't
there when you entered, or if you attempt to access a restricted vault area instead of just
exiting after a deposit, the guard will immediately flag this. They look at the content (the
bag) and the behavior (going to the vault instead of exiting) within the context of your
established visit.
If someone else (an unknown IP) tries to leave pretending to be you, or if an "exit" doesn't
match an "entry" in their log, they are stopped.

Working of Stateful Inspection
Connection Initiation (e.g., You open a website):
•Your computer (internal IP) sends a request packet to a website server (external
IP) on a specific port (e.g., 443 for HTTPS).
•The firewall intercepts this first packet.
•It sees this is a newconnection request.
State Table Entry Creation:
The firewall creates an entry in its state table for this specific connection. This entry
records details like:
•Source IP & Port (your computer)
•Destination IP & Port (the website server)
•Protocol (TCP, UDP)
•Current state of the connection (e.g., "connection pending," "established,"
"closing").

•The firewall intercepts this first packet.

•Decision Based on Rules:

6. Cloud firewall
•A cloud firewall is a network security solution that is specifically designed to
protect cloud -based resources and applications .
•It serves as a barrier between an organization's infrastructure hosted in the
cloud and external threats, such as unauthorized access, malicious attacks,
and data breaches.
•Cloud firewalls provide security for virtualized environments and are often an
integral part of cloud security strategies .
Benefits of Cloud firewall
•Unified security policy
•Flexible deployment
•Simplified deployment and maintenance
•Improved scalability
•Automatic updates

Modes of NAT ( Static NAT )
Static NAT: it involves mapping a private IP address to a specific public IP
address on a one -to-one basis. This creates a permanent association between a
private and public IP address.
Example :
Private IP:192.168.1.10
Public IP:203.0.113.5
Static NAT mapping :192.168.1.10(private) ->203.0.113.5(public)
Use:Static NAT isoften used when aserver inside aprivate network needs tobe
accessible from theinternet, such asaweb server oranFTP server .

[Page 35]
Modes of NAT ( Port Address Translation )
PAT (Port Address Translation) or NAT Overload: PAT maps multiple private IP
addresses to a single public IP address using different port numbers for each connection.
This allows many devices to share the same public IP address simultaneously.
Example:
Private IP 1: 192.168.1.100
Private IP 2: 192.168.1.101
Public IP: 203.0.113.2
PAT mapping: Private IP 1 -> 203.0.113.2:5001, Private IP 2 -> 203.0.113.2:5002
Use Case : PAT is commonly used in home or small office networks where multiple devices
share a single public IP address to access the internet simultaneously.

How Snort Works?
The basic process ofhow Snort works canbebroken down into three main stages :packet
capture, detection, andalerting/logging .
Packet Capture :Snort first "listens" toallthenetwork traffic passing through aspecific
point, much likeawiretap .Itcaptures these individual data packets foranalysis .
Detection Engine :The captured packets arefedinto thedetection engine .Here, they are
compared against asetofpredefined rules .The rules arelike alistofknown suspicious
behaviors or"wanted" posters fornetwork threats .
Alerts &Logging :Ifapacket matches arule, Snort triggers anevent .Itcanlogtheincident
toafile, send analert toanadministrator, oreven drop themalicious packet depending on
how it'sconfigured .

Snort Rules
« Snort has several types of rules that affect how it handles traffic:
« Alert rules - Log packets whose characteristics match a predefined suspicious
pattern (e.g., generated by a common hacking tool, or contain a string indicative of
a buffer overflow or web attack) or custom rules that monitor packets you
determine to be prohibited or undesirable on your network (e.g., file sharing,
gaming, etc.).
« Pass rules - Explicitly ignore packets. Traffic that matches these rules will not be
logged.
« Log rules - Record packets but do not generate rules. This would be useful for
diagnosing network problems, storing traffic for audits, or monitoring sensitive
systems so that traffic can be analyzed in case a compromise is detected.

[Page 31]
Traditional Problems Associated with Computer Crime
4.Lack ofReporting :
•Many victims (especially companies) do not report cybercrimes.
Reasons:
•Fear of losing customer trust.
•Desire to keep control of the investigation internally.
•Belief that reporting won’t help catch the criminal.
•Sometimes the crime is noticed too late.
Example :In 2013, Yahoo suffered a massive data breach affecting 3 billion accounts,
but it was only reported years later. The delay raised questions about transparency and
consumer trust.

[Page 37]
INCIDENT RESPONSE
During a cyber incident, the Incident Response Team should focus on three main things :
1.Endpoint Analysis (Checking the devices )
•Look at the computers or devices that were attacked .
•Find the “footprints” or traces left behind by the hacker .
•Make a complete copy of the system to carefully study what exactly happened on that
device .
2.Binary Analysis (Checking the hacker’s tools )
•Study the malicious files, programs, or tools the hacker used .
•Find out what those tools do, like stealing data, creating backdoors, or spreading viruses .
This can be done in two ways :
Behavior Analysis –running the program safely in a test environment to see what it does .
Static Analysis –looking at the code or structure without running it.

Contemporary Crimes (Modern Cybercrimes)
These are crimes that are common today in the digital era :
Identity theft –Stealing personal info to commit fraud.
•Example: In 2021, several Indians lost money in KYC fraud calls , where scammers pretended to
be from banks .
Online fraud / phishing –Fake emails/websites to trick users.
•Example: Amazon/ Paytm phishing scams in India where fake links stole credit card details .
Cyberstalking & harassment –Using the internet to threaten or harass people.
•Example: Bollywood celebrities often face social media abuse and police cyber cells take action .
Ransomware attacks –Locking data until money is paid.
•Example: WannaCry ransomware attack (2017) affected hospitals, banks, and companies
worldwide, including India’s railways.

Indian IT Act 2000
The ITAct2000 stands fortheInformation Technology Act, 2000 .ItisanIndian law
passed bytheGovernment ofIndia toprovide alegal framework forelectronic
transactions ,e-commerce, andcybercrimes .
Key Points ofITAct2000 :
1.Came into effect –17thOctober 2000 .
2.Purpose –Toencourage theuseofelectronic records andprevent cybercrimes .
3.Legal Recognition –Digital signatures andelectronic records areconsidered valid like
paper documents .
4.Cybercrimes Covered –Hacking, data theft, virus attacks, cyber terrorism, publishing
obscene content, identity theft, etc.
5.Authorities Established –
1.Controller ofCertifying Authorities (fordigital signatures) .
2.Cyber Appellate Tribunal (forresolving cyber disputes) .

Types of Firewalls
•Packet -filtering firewall –Checks each packet’s source, destination, andport, then
allows orblocks it.
•Stateful Multi -Layer Inspection (SMLI) –Tracks thestate ofactive connections to
make smarter filtering decisions .
•Stateless firewall –Examines packets individually without remembering past
traffic (faster butlesssecure) .
•Application -level gateway (Proxy firewall) –Acts asamiddleman, filtering traffic
attheapplication layer .

•Stateful Multi -Layer Inspection (SMLI) –Tracks thestate ofactive connections to
make smarter filtering decisions .
•Stateless firewall –Examines packets individually without remembering past
traffic (faster butlesssecure) .
•Application -level gateway (Proxy firewall) –Acts asamiddleman, filtering traffic
attheapplication layer .

Example of Packet Filtering Firewalls
Packet filters examine each TCP/IP packet, looking atthesource anddestination IP
andport addresses .You cancreate rules thatallow only known andestablished IP
addresses while blocking allunknown orunknown IPaddresses .
Denying outsiders access toport 80,forexample, would prevent alloutside access
totheHTTP server, because most HTTP servers runonport 80.You canalso
configure your firewall toallow only packets intended foryour mail orweb server
while rejecting allothers .

How to Tell if You Have Spyware
Clues your system may be infected:
•Sudden slow performance or frequent crashes.
•Unwanted ads/pop -ups, even when offline.
•Homepage or search engine changed without permission.
•Unknown apps/processes running in Task Manager.
•High internet/data usage when you’re not actively online.
•Battery drain (on mobiles) and overheating.
•Suspicious browser toolbars or extensions you didn’t install.
•Your accounts hacked or logins attempted from strange locations.

4Introduction
•A Group Discussion (GD) is a selection process where candidates
discuss a topic in a group setting.
•Evaluates communication skills, knowledge, leadership, teamwork,
and analytical thinking.
•Unlike personal interviews, GDs assess interaction with peers and
the ability to present ideas under observation.
•Commonly used in campus placements, competitive exams, and
recruitment drives.

Types of GD Topics
•Current Affairs: National/global issues, e.g., “Impact of AI on
employment.”
•Abstract Topics: Conceptual topics, e.g., “Innovation is the key to
success.”
•Case Studies / Situation-Based: Practical scenarios, e.g., “A company
faces declining sales—what would you suggest?”
•Technical / Domain-Specific : Engineering, science, or tech topics,
e.g., “Future of electric vehicles in India.”

Key Skills for Effective GD
•Communication Skills: Speak clearly, concisely, and professionally.
•Analytical Thinking: Present logical arguments and relevant facts.
•Teamwork: Respect others’ opinions, avoid conflicts.
•Leadership: Initiate discussion, summarize key points, moderate
debates.
•Time Management: Speak within your turn, avoid monopolizing
the discussion.

Do’s in GD
•Listen attentively to all participants.
•Speak clearly and confidently.
•Support arguments with facts, statistics, or examples .
•Maintain positive body language and professional demeanor.
•Politely take initiative to start or guide discussion .

Don’ts in GD

•Do not use informal language, slang, or filler words .

[Page 11]
SPELT Approach (Social, Political, Economic, Legal, Technological)
SPELT helps analyze topics from multiple perspectives for effective GD participation:
•S – Social: Evaluate the impact on society, people, lifestyle, culture, and employment.
•P – Political: Examine government policies, regulations, and political implications.
•E – Economic: Assess financial, business, and economic outcomes such as costs, profits, or
investments.
•L – Legal: Consider laws, compliance requirements, ethics, and regulations.
•T – Technological: Analyze innovations, tools, and technological feasibility.
Tip: Mapping points across these dimensions ensures a well-rounded, structured contribution
during discussions.

[Page 12]
SPELT Example
Topic: “Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare”
•Social: Improves patient care, reduces human error, enhances accessibility.
•Political: Government policies and regulations for AI implementation in hospitals.
•Economic: Reduces costs, creates investment opportunities in AI startups.
•Legal: Compliance with patient data privacy laws and ethical guidelines.
•Technological: AI diagnostic tools, predictive analytics, and machine learning
algorithms.
Tip: Speak on 2–3 points under each perspective, use examples/facts , and maintain
clarity during your turn.

[Page 13]
Keyword Approach
•Identify keywords from the topic to guide discussion.
•Helps organize thoughts quickly under time pressure.
•Ensures discussion remains relevant and focused .
•Reduces the chance of digression or filler content .
Example: Topic: “Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare” → Keywords: AI,
diagnosis, efficiency, patient care, technology.

Role of Leadership in GD
•Take initiative to start or steer a discussion politely .
•Encourage quieter members to share their opinions .
•Summarize key discussion points .
•Balance speaking and listening for group harmony.
Example: Leading a discussion on “Electric vehicles: Challenges and
Opportunities.”

Steps for Interview Preparation
Self-assessment of strengths, weaknesses, and goals
Prepare a strong elevator pitch
Research the company (mission, culture, services)
Understand the job role and key responsibilities
Align skills with the job description
Review and be thorough with your resume
Prepare real-life examples to support resume points
Stay updated with industry trends and news
Maintain professional interview etiquette
Dress appropriately and be punctual

•"Why do you want to join us?"
•"What is your leadership style?"
Why Use PEEL?
•Useful for structured verbal & written answers
•Makes arguments persuasive & clear
•Helps connect points logically
Example
•Point: I am a collaborative leader.
•Evidence: In my last internship, I led a team of 5...
•Explanation: This helped us achieve...
•Link: I believe collaboration drives innovation here too.

candidates.

•The Choice: Candidates are given choice to select their format, Paper-Based or Computer-

•Skills Assessed: Understanding main ideas, specific factual information, speaker opinions,
and following the development of an argument.

[Page 9]
Completion Tasks
•Form/Note/Table/Sentence Completion: Filling in gaps using words from the recording.
Adhere strictly to the word limit (e.g., 'NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/OR A
NUMBER').
Selection Tasks
•Multiple Choice: Choosing one or more correct answers from a list.
•Matching: Matching items (e.g., names) to options (e.g., actions).
Visual Tasks
•Plan/Map/Diagram Labeling: Relating descriptive language (directions, location) to a
visual.
Strategy Tip: Read the instructions and the questions before the recording starts to
predict the context and the type of answer needed (e.g., a name, a date, a number).

Strategy 2: Listen for Paraphrasing
•The words you hear in the recording are often synonyms or paraphrases of the words in the question.
•Example: Question says "near future," audio says "in a couple of weeks."
•Listen for the whole meaning , not just a single word match.
Strategy 3: Don't Panic & Move On
•The Answers are in Order: If you miss Question 5, you know the answer to Question 6 will come next in the
audio.
•Stay in Sync: Don't waste time thinking about a missed answer; that will cause you to miss the next one. Guess
and move your attention to the next question immediately.

Multiple-Choice Questions (MCQ):
•Requires you to listen for details and distractors.
•The speaker often mentions all options, but only one will be the final or correct piece of
information.
•Listen for words like "but," "however," or "actually“ - these often signal the real answer.
Matching:
•You match items from a list (e.g., people, places) to a set of options (e.g., opinions, features).
•Focus on identifying the speaker's core opinion or a specific link between the items.

Final 10 Minutes (Transfer Time):
•This time is only for transferring answers from your test booklet to the official answer sheet.
•Do NOT use it to try and guess missing answers; your focus must be on accuracy.
Crucial Accuracy Checks:
•Spelling: Every answer must be spelled 100% correctly . Practice common subject-specific words.
•Grammar: Your answer must fit grammatically into the sentence or form. (e.g., using a singular
vs. plural noun).
•Plurals: Listen closely for the 'S' sound; if the answer is plural, you must write the 'S'.

The General Training Reading test is assessed by certified markers
who undergo regular monitoring to ensure reliability. Following the
initial marking, all answer sheets are additionally analyzed by
Cambridge Assessment English.
Band Score Conversion
 For each version of the General Training Reading test, a band score
conversion table is generated. This table translates scores, ranging
from 0 to 40, into the IELTS 9-band scale. Results are reported in
both whole bands and half bands.

Top 5 Tips to Ace IELTS Speaking
Dedicate 15-20 minutes each day to speaking on diverse topics.
Use suitable technical vocabulary to impart expertise, but always
explain concepts in easy manner
Follow a logical format: introduction, detailed explanation, relevant
example, and conclusion. This demonstrates coherence and
organization
Use your phone to record practice sessions. Listen back to identify
pronunciation issues, filler words, and areas for fluency
improvement
Maintain eye contact, smile, and don't panic if you make mistakes .

Key Language Features of IELTS
Writing
1. Academic Vocabulary
 Use precise, sophisticated terms appropriate for/9+*/formal
writing. Avoid repetition through synonyms
2. Complex Sentences
 Employ relative clauses, conditionals, and subordinate clauses
to demonstrate grammatical range.
3. Cohesive Devices
 Connect ideas logically using "therefore," "in addition to," "on
the other hand," or "consequently."

Essentials of Report Writing
Paraphrase the question and describe what the visual
shows (Introduction)
Summarize the main trends, patterns, or key features
(Overview)
Present specific data points and comparisons
systematically (Detailed Analysis)

[Page 8]
Tips for Report Writing
1. Analyze the Visual
Identify main trends, highest/lowest points, and any exceptions or
anomalies in the data.
2. Paraphrase the Question
Rewrite the question in your own words for the introduction—never
copy directly.
3. Select Data Strategically
Include key figures to support trends—don't describe every single
data point.
4. Maintain Formal Tone
Keep language objective, precise, and academic throughout your
report.

[Page 10]
1. Opinion Essays
Present and justify your viewpoint on a given statement with clear
arguments and examples
2. Discussion Essays
Explore both sides of an argument fairly before presenting your own
balanced conclusion
3. Problem/Solution
Identify problems clearly and propose practical, well-reasoned solutions
with supporting evidence
4. Cause/Effect
Analyze reasons behind issues and their consequences, demonstrating
logical reasoning

2. Plan Your Essay

vocabulary.
4. Support Your Ideas
Back up arguments with relevant examples, evidence, or logical reasoning
throughout.

[Page 12]
1. Word Count Errors
Writing under or over the required word count (150 for Task and 1250 for
Task 2) will lower your score.
2. Copying From Prompt
Directly copying phrases from the question without paraphrasing
demonstrates limited language ability.
3. Going Off-Topic
Failing to address the question directly task achievement scores.
4. Poor Time Management
Spending too long on Task 1 leaves insufficient time for more heavily
weighted Task 2.

High Performance Computing – Additional Exam-Oriented Questions (16 Marks)
UNIT 1 – INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL COMPUTING
1. Explain the limitations of memory system performance and how they influence parallel
computing.
2. Discuss different levels of parallelism with real-world examples.
3. Compare SIMD, MIMD, SIMT, SPMD, and Dataflow computational models.
4. Explain communication costs in parallel machines in detail.
UNIT 2 – PARALLEL ALGORITHM DESIGN
1. Describe decomposition techniques with suitable examples.
2. Explain characteristics of tasks and interactions in parallel algorithms.
3. Discuss static and dynamic mapping techniques for load balancing.
4. Explain methods for reducing interaction overheads.
UNIT 3 – MESSAGE PASSING PARADIGM
1. Explain blocking vs non-blocking message passing with examples.
2. Discuss MPI communicators and their significance.
3. Describe MPI collective communication operations in detail.
4. Explain how computation can be overlapped with communication in MPI.
UNIT 4 – SYNCHRONIZATION
1. Explain different synchronization primitives in Pthreads.
2. Discuss job scheduling and dependency analysis in parallel systems.
3. Explain thread cancellation and composite synchronization constructs.
4. Describe OpenMP parallel constructs with examples.
UNIT 5 – GPGPU & CUDA PROGRAMMING
1. Explain the SIMT model and GPU thread hierarchy.
2. Describe the CUDA memory hierarchy and its performance implications.
3. Discuss the DGX architecture and its relevance to HPC.
4. Explain steps involved in writing and launching a CUDA kernel.
UNIT 6 – PERFORMANCE MEASURES
1. Explain speedup, efficiency, and scalability with numerical examples.
2. Describe the concept of work and critical path with diagrams.
3. Explain Amdahl’s Law and its implications on scalability.
4. Compare abstract vs real performance factors in HPC systems.

•The speed of an application is determined by more than just
processor speed.
–Memory speed
–Disk speed
–Network speed
•Multiprocessors typically improve the aggregate speeds:
–Memory bandwidth is improved by separate memories.
–Multiprocessors usually have more aggregate cache memory.
–Each processor in a cluster can have its own disk and network adapter,
improving aggregate speeds.
•Communication enables parallel applications:
–Harnessing the computing power of distributed systems over the
Internet is a popular example of parallel processing.
•Constraints on the location of data
–Huge data sets could be difficult, expensive, or otherwise infeasible to
store in a central location.
–Distributed data and parallel processing is a practical solution.

Moore's law states [1965]:
``The complexity for minimum component
costs has increased at a rate of roughly a factor of two
per year. Certainly over the short term this rate can be
expected to continue, if not to increase. Over the longer
term, the rate of increase is a bit more uncertain,
although there is no reason to believe it will not remain
nearly constant for at least 10 years. That means by
1975, the number of components per integrated circuit
for minimum cost will be 65,000.''

Moore attributed this doubling rate to exponential behavior of
die sizes, finer minimum dimensions, and ``circuit and device
cleverness''.
In 1975, he revised this law as follows:
``There is no room left to squeeze anything out
by being clever. Going forward from here we have to depend
on the two size factors -bigger dies and finer dimensions.''

doubling to 18 months and projected from 1975 onwards at
this reduced rate. The Computational Power Argument

•It is very important to understand the difference between
latency and bandwidth.
•Consider the example of a fire -hose. If the water comes out of
the hose two seconds after the hydrant is turned on, the
latency of the system is two seconds.
•Once the water starts flowing, if the hydrant delivers water at
the rate of 5 gallons/second, the bandwidth of the system is 5
gallons/second.
•If you want immediate response from the hydrant, it is
important to reduce latency.
•If you want to fight big fires, you want high bandwidth. Memory System Performance: Bandwidth and Latency

Preliminaries: Decomposition, Tasks, and
Dependency Graphs
• The first step in developing a parallel algorithm is to decompose the problem into tasks that can be executed concurrently
• A given problem may be decomposed into tasks in many different ways.
• Tasks may be of same, different, or even indeterminate sizes.
• A decomposition can be illustrated in the form of a directed graph.
–Such a graph is called a task-dependency graph .
–Nodes correspond to tasks and edges indicate dependencies

Granularity of Task Decompositions
•The number of tasks into which a problem is decomposed
determines its granularity.
–Fine granularity : Decomposition into a large number of tasks
–Coarse granularity : Decomposition into a small number of tasks

Degree of Concurrency
•The number of tasks that can be executed in parallel is the degree
of concurrency of a decomposition.
•Since the number of tasks that can be executed in parallel may
change over program execution, the maximum degree of
concurrency is the maximum number of such tasks at any point
during execution.
•The average degree of concurrency is the average number of tasks
that can be processed in parallel over the execution of the program.
•The degree of concurrency increases as the decomposition
becomes finer in granularity and vice versa.

Limits on Parallel Performance
•Parallel time cannot be made arbitrarily small by making the decomposition
finer in granularity.
–A parallel algorithm will inherently have a limited number of decomposable tasks
–For example, in the case of multiplying a dense matrix with a vector, there can be
no more than (n2)concurrent tasks.
•Tasks interaction is another limiting factor on parallel performance
•Task interaction graph : an undirected graph that captures the pattern of
interactions among tasks
•Note that task interaction graphs represent data dependencies, whereas
task dependency graphs represent control dependencies.

Processes and Mapping
•In general, the number of tasks in a decomposition exceeds the
number of processing elements available.
–Thus, a parallel algorithm must also provide a mapping of tasks to
processes.
•Note: mapping is from tasks to processes, as opposed to processors.
–Because typical programming APIs do not allow easy binding of tasks to physical
processors.
–We aggregate tasks into processes and rely on the system to map these
processes to physical processors.

Input Data Partitioning
•Output data partitioning is applicable if each output can be naturally
computed as a function of the input.
•In many algorithms it is not possible or desirable to partition the
output data
–e.g., the problem of finding the minimum in a list, sorting a given list, etc.
•In such cases, it is sometimes possible to partition the input data,
and then use this partitioning to induce concurrency
•A task is associated with each input data partition. The task
performs as much of the computation with its part of the data.
Subsequent processing combines these partial results.

The Owner Computes Rule
•The Owner Computes Rule generally states that the process
assigned a particular data item is responsible for all computation
associated with it.

•In the case of input data decomposition, the owner computes rule
implies that all computations that use the input data are performed
by the process.
•In the case of output data decomposition, the owner computes rule
implies that the output is computed by the process to which the
output data is assigned.

Speculative Decomposition
•Used to extract concurrency in problems in which the next step is
one of many possible actions that can only be determined when the
current tasks finishes
•This decomposition assumes a certain outcome of the currently
executed task and executes some of the next steps
–Just like speculative execution at the microprocessor level
•Performs more or the same aggregate work (but not less) than the
sequential algorithm

Cyclic and Block Cyclic Distributions
•If the amount of computation associated with data items varies, a
block decomposition may lead to significant load imbalances.
•A simple example of this is in LU decomposition (or Gaussian
Elimination) of dense matrices.
•Block cyclic distribution schemes can be used to alleviate the load -
imbalance and idling problems.
•Block cyclic distributions:
–Partition an array into many more blocks than the number of available
processes.
–Blocks are assigned to processes in a round -robin manner so that each
process gets several non -adjacent blocks.

Centralized Dynamic Mapping
•Processes are designated as masters or slaves.

[Page 58]
Centralized Dynamic Mapping
•Processes are designated as masters or slaves.
•When a process runs out of work, it requests the master for more work.
•When the number of processes increases, the master may become the
bottleneck.
•To alleviate this, a process may pick up a number of tasks (a chunk) at one
time. This is called Chunk scheduling.
•Selecting large chunk sizes may lead to significant load imbalances as well.
•A number of schemes have been used to gradually decrease chunk size as
the computation progresses.

Distributed Dynamic Mapping
•Each process can send or receive work from other processes.
•This alleviates the bottleneck in centralized schemes.
•There are four critical questions:
–How are sending and receiving processes paired together,
–Who initiates work transfer,
–How much work is transferred, and
–When is a transfer triggered?

Methods for Containing Interaction Overheads
•Maximize data locality : Where possible, reuse intermediate data.
Restructure computation so that data can be reused in smaller time
windows.
•Minimize volume of data exchange : There is a cost associated
with each word that is communicated. For this reason, we must
minimize the volume of data communicated.
•Minimize frequency of interactions : There is a startup cost
associated with each interaction. Therefore, try to merge multiple
interactions to one, where possible.
•Minimize contention and hot -spots : Use decentralized
techniques, replicate data where necessary.

Parallel Algorithm Models
•An algorithm model is a way of structuring a parallel algorithm by selecting
a decomposition and mapping technique and applying the appropriate
strategy to minimize interactions.
•Data Parallel Model : Tasks are statically (or semi -statically) mapped to
processes and each task performs similar operations on different data.
–Usually based on data decomposition followed by static mapping
–Uniform partitioning of data followed by static mapping guarantees load balance
–Example algorithm: dense matrix multiplication
•Task Graph Model : Starting from a task dependency graph, the
interrelationships among the tasks are utilized to promote locality or to
reduce interaction costs.
–Typically used to solve problems where amount of data associated with a task is
large relative to computation
–Static mapping usually used to optimize data movement costs
–Example algorithm: parallel quicksort, sparse matrix factorization

Parallel Algorithm Models

allocate it to worker processes. This allocation may be static or
dynamic.
•Pipeline / Producer -Consumer Model : A stream of data is passed
through a succession of processes, each of which perform some
task on it.
•Hybrid Models : A hybrid model may be composed either of multiple
models applied hierarchically or multiple models applied
sequentially to different phases of a parallel algorithm.

[Page 94]
Comparison with other CPU
•Based on the much simpler Pentium P54C design.
•Each Larrabee core contains a 512 -bit vector processing unit, able
to process 16 single precision floating point numbers at a time.
•Larrabee includes one major fixed -function graphics hardware
feature: texture sampling units.
•Larrabee has a 1024 -bit (512 -bit each way) ring bus for
communication between cores and to memory.
•Larrabee includes explicit cache control instructions.
•Each core supports 4 -way simultaneous multithreading, with 4
copies of each processor register.

[Page 95]
New ISA
•512 bit vector types ( 8×64bit double, 16×32bit float, or 16×32bit
integer)

Overview
•Scalar Processing Unit
•Vector Processing Unit
•Separated Register File

Here is the block diagram of one Larrabee core.
Basically, there are two Processing Unit in each
cores: scalar processing unit and a vector processing
unit. Although the two processing unit are located
within the same core, they have completely separate
register files. Moreover, there are not direct channel
between scalar unit and vector unit, the nearest
sharing module is L1 cache. That’s to say, the two
units can not talk to each other unless using the
system memory. The communication overhead
maybe varied. If the shared memory is located in
cache, the communication cost will be negligible.

memory hierarchy.

Principles of
Message -Passing Programming
•The logical view of a machine supporting the message -
passing paradigm consists of pprocesses, each with its
own exclusive address space.
•There are two immediate implementations of a
partitioned address space.
•First, Each data element must belong to one of the
partitions of the space; hence, data must be explicitly
partitioned and placed.
•Second, All interactions (read -only or read/write) require
cooperation of two processes -the process that has the
data and the process that wants to access the data.
•These two constraints, while onerous, make underlying
costs very explicit to the programmer.

Principles of
Message -Passing Programming
•Message -passing programs are often written using the
asynchronous or loosely synchronous paradigms.
•In the asynchronous paradigm, all concurrent tasks
execute asynchronously.
•In the loosely synchronous model, tasks or subsets of
tasks synchronize to perform interactions. Between
these interactions, tasks execute completely
asynchronously.
•Most message -passing programs are written using the
single program multiple data (SPMD) model.

protocols.

Blocking Message passing Operation
Non -Buffered Blocking
Message Passing Operations
•A simple method for forcing send/receive semantics is for the send
operation to return only when it is safe to do so.
•In the non -buffered blocking send, the operation does not return until
the matching receive has been encountered at the receiving
process.
•Idling and deadlocks are major issues with non -buffered blocking
sends.
•In buffered blocking sends, the sender simply copies the data into
the designated buffer and returns after the copy operation has been
completed. The data is copied at a buffer at the receiving end as
well.
•Buffering alleviates idling at the expense of copying overheads.

Buffered Blocking
Message Passing Operations
•A simple solution to the idling and deadlocking problem
outlined above is to rely on buffers at the sending and
receiving ends.
•The sender simply copies the data into the designated
buffer and returns after the copy operation has been
completed.
•The data must be buffered at the receiving end as well.
•Buffering trades off idling overhead for buffer copying
overhead.

[Page 17]
Starting and Terminating the MPI Library
•MPI_Init is called prior to any calls to other MPI routines. Its
purpose is to initialize the MPI environment.
•MPI_Finalize is called at the end of the computation, and it
performs various clean -up tasks to terminate the MPI environment.
•The prototypes of these two functions are:
int MPI_Init(int *argc, char ***argv)
int MPI_Finalize()
•MPI_Init also strips off any MPI related command -line arguments.
•All MPI routines, data -types, and constants are prefixed by “ MPI_”.
The return code for successful completion is MPI_SUCCESS .

•A communicator defines a communication domain -a set
of processes that are allowed to communicate with each
other.

variables of type MPI_Comm .
•Communicators are used as arguments to all message
transfer MPI routines.
•A process can belong to many different (possibly
overlapping) communication domains.
•MPI defines a default communicator called
MPI_COMM_WORLD which includes all the processes.

Sending and Receiving Messages
•The basic functions for sending and receiving messages in MPI are
the MPI_Send and MPI_Recv , respectively.
•The calling sequences of these routines are as follows:
int MPI_Send(void *buf, int count, MPI_Datatype
datatype, int dest, int tag, MPI_Comm comm)
int MPI_Recv(void *buf, int count, MPI_Datatype
datatype, int source, int tag,
MPI_Comm comm, MPI_Status *status)
•MPI provides equivalent datatypes for all C datatypes. This is done
for portability reasons.
•The datatype MPI_BYTE corresponds to a byte (8 bits) and
MPI_PACKED corresponds to a collection of data items that has
been created by packing non -contiguous data.
•The message -tag can take values ranging from zero up to the MPI
defined constant MPI_TAG_UB .

source and tag.

of the communication domain can be the source of the
message.
•If tag is set to MPI_ANY_TAG , then messages with any
tag are accepted.

equal to or less than the length field specified.

Avoiding Deadlocks
Consider the following piece of code, in which process i

processes) and receives a message from process i-1
(module the number of processes).

...

MPI_COMM_WORLD);
MPI_Recv(b, 10, MPI_INT, (myrank -1+npes)%npes, 1,
MPI_COMM_WORLD);
...
Once again, we have a deadlock if MPI_Send is blocking .

Topologies and Embeddings
•MPI allows a programmer to organize processors into
logical k-d meshes.
•The processor ids in MPI_COMM_WORLD can be mapped
to other communicators (corresponding to higher -
dimensional meshes) in many ways.
•The goodness of any such mapping is determined by the
interaction pattern of the underlying program and the
topology of the machine.
•MPI does not provide the programmer any control over
these mappings.

Groups and Communicators
•In many parallel algorithms, communication operations
need to be restricted to certain subsets of processes.
•MPI provides mechanisms for partitioning the group of
processes that belong to a communicator into subgroups
each corresponding to a different communicator.
•The simplest such mechanism is:
int MPI_Comm_split(MPI_Comm comm, int color, int key,
MPI_Comm *newcomm)
•This operation groups processors by color and sorts
resulting groups on the key.

Groups and Communicators
•In many parallel algorithms, processes are arranged in a virtual grid,
and in different steps of the algorithm, communication needs to be
restricted to a different subset of the grid.
•MPI provides a convenient way to partition a Cartesian topology to
form lower -dimensional grids:
int MPI_Cart_sub(MPI_Comm comm_cart, int *keep_dims,
MPI_Comm *comm_subcart)
•If keep_dims[i] is true (non -zero value in C) then the ith dimension
is retained in the new sub -topology.
•The coordinate of a process in a sub -topology created by
MPI_Cart_sub can be obtained from its coordinate in the original
topology by disregarding the coordinates that correspond to the
dimensions that were not retained.

asetof4to16Nvidia Tesla GPU modules onanindependent system

theSXM socket .

- Web Development
- Web development is the process of creating, building, and maintaining websites or web
- applications that run on the internet. It involves designing the user interface, writing application
- logic, managing databases, and deploying applications on web servers.
- Frontend Development
- e Concerned with the user interface (Ul) and user experience (UX)
- e What users see and interact with in the browser
- e Runs on the client side
- Common technologies: HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Angular, React
- e
- Examples: page layout, buttons, forms, colors, animations
- Backend Development
- e Concerned with server-side logic and data handling
- e Manages databases, authentication, APIs, and business rules
- e Runs on the server
- e Common technologies: Node.js, Express.js, Java, Python, PHP, MongoDB
- Examples: user login validation, storing data, fetching records from database
- Difference Between Frontend and Backend

- Client-Server Architecture is a computing model in which a client (usually a web browser)

- an appropriate response
- In web applications:

- Q. 10 Explain the configuration files used in MongoDB.
- MongoDB uses a configuration file to control how the MongoDB server runs. The default
- configuration file is mongod.conf.
- Purpose of mongod.conf
- e Defines server settings and behavior
- e Helps in customizing MongoDB without command-line options
- Important Configuration Options
- Storage: database path (dbPath)
- System Log: log file location
- Network: port number and IP binding
- Security: authentication and authorization
- Replication: replica set settings

- documents faster without scanning the entire collection.

- e Speeds up query execution
- e Improves performance for large collections
- Reduces disk I/O operations

- e Makes sorting and searching efficient

- Q. 20 What aggregation pipelines in MongoDB?
- are
- Solution
- MongoDB uses aggregation pipelines to process, transform, and analyze data stored in
- collections. An aggregation pipeline is a step-by-step data processing framework where
- documents pass through multiple stages, and each stage performs a specific operation.
- Think of it like an assembly line:
- data enters — gets filtered — grouped — transformed — final result comes out.
- Why Aggregation Pipelines Are Used
- To perform data analysis and reporting
- e
- e To transform raw data into meaningful results
- To perform operations similar to SQL GROUP BY
- e To process large datasets efficiently
- How Aggregation Pipeline Works
- e Data flows through a sequence of stages
- e Each stage processes the output of the previous stage
- e The final stage returns the result

- 1. $match
- Filters documents (similar to WHERE in SQL).
- db.orders.aggregate([

- Best Practices
- Design Based on Application Queries
- Structure data according to how the application reads and writes data.
- Use Embedding When Possible
- Embed related data for faster read performance.
- Use Referencing for Large Shared Data
- or
- Reference documents when data is large or frequently reused.
- Avoid Deeply Nested Documents
- Keep document depth minimal for better performance.
- Optimize Index Usage
- Create indexes on frequently queried fields.
- Balance Data Duplication
- Allow controlled duplication to improve read speed.
- Plan for Scalability
- Design schema keeping future data growth in mind.

- mobile apps.

Authentication

are

to

and
resources.
Authentication
is
the
process
of
verifying
the
identity
of
a
user.
It
checks
whether
the
user
is
genuine
or
not.
This
is
usually
done
by
asking
for
credentials
such
as
a
username
and
password,
OTP,
or
biometric
verification.
If
the
provided
details
are
correct,
the
user
is
successfully
authenticated.
For
example,
when
a
user
logs
into
an
application
using
email
and
password,
the
system
verifies
the
credentials
before
allowing
access.
Authorization,
on
the
other
hand,
is
the
process
of
determining
what
an
authenticated
user
is
allowed
to
do.
It
defines
the
permissions
or
access
rights
of
a
user.
After
authentication,
the
system
checks
whether
the
user
has
permission
to
access
a
specific
resource
or

a
particular
action.

example,
in
an
admin
panel:

admin
can

update,
and
delete
users.
●
A
normal
user
can
only
view
their
profile.

simple
terms:
●
Authentication
answers
the
question:
“Who
are
you?”
●
Authorization
answers
the
question:
“What
are
you
allowed
to
do?”
Thus,
authentication
verifies
identity,
while
authorization
controls
access,
and
both
are
essential
for
securing
web
applications.

- Q 14 What are sessions in Express.js?
- Solution
- Sessions in Express.js are used to store user-specific data on the server side across multiple
- HTTP requests. Since HTTP is a stateless protocol, each request is independent. Sessions help
- maintain user state, such as login information, shopping cart data, or user preferences.

- JWT (JSON Web Token) is token-based authentication mechanism used in Node.js
- a
- applications to securely transmit information between client and server. It is commonly used for
- stateless authentication in RESTful APIs.
- A JWT consists of three parts separated by dots (.):
- e Header — Contains the token type and signing algorithm.
- e Payload Contains user data (such as user ID, email, role).
- —
- e Signature — Ensures the token is secure and not tampered with, created using a secret
- key.
- The structure looks like:

- Working of JWT Authentication:
- 4 User Login:
- The user sends login credentials (username and password) to the server.
- 2. Token Generation:
- If credentials are valid, the server generates a JWT using a secret key and sends it back to the
- client.
- 3. Token Storage:
- The client stores the token (usually in localStorage or cookies).
- 4. Accessing Protected Routes:
- For subsequent requests, the client sends the token in the Authorization header:
- Authorization: Bearer <token>
- 5. Token Verification:
- The server verifies the token using the secret key.
- e If valid — access is granted.
- e If invalid or expired — access is denied.
- Advantages of JWT:
- Stateless (no need to store sessions the server)
- e on
- e Scalable for large applications
- Secure and compact
- e
- Suitable for REST APIs and mobile applications
- Thus, JWT authentication in Node.js provides a secure and efficient way to manage user
- authentication without maintaining server-side sessions.

Explain Angular framework. What are its key features and advantages in web development? Solution Angular Framework Angular is a TypeScript-based open-source front-end web application framework developed and maintained by Google. It is used to build dynamic, scalable, and single-page web applications (SPAs). Angular provides a structured approach to building web applications by dividing the application into components, modules, and services, which makes development easier and more organized. Angular mainly uses HTML, CSS, and TypeScript to create interactive user interfaces. Architecture of Angular The Angular framework follows a component-based architecture. Main building blocks include: ● Components – Control the view and UI logic. ● Modules (NgModule) – Organize the application into logical parts. ● Templates – HTML used to display data. ● Services – Provide reusable business logic and data handling. ● Directives – Extend HTML functionality. ● Dependency Injection – Manage service objects efficiently. Components Q 8. What is an Angular Component? Explain its structure with an example. Solution Angular Component An Angular Component is the basic building block of an application developed using the Angular framework. A component controls a part of the user interface (UI) and defines how that part of the application behaves. Each Angular application is made up of multiple components that work together to build the complete user interface. For example: ● Header component ● Login component ● Dashboard component ● Footer component Each component manages its own logic, template, and style. Explain the lifecycle hooks of Angular components. Solution Lifecycle Hooks of Angular Components In Angular, Lifecycle Hooks are special methods that allow developers to execute code at different stages of a component's life. These stages include creation, update, and destruction of a component. Lifecycle hooks help developers control and manage component behavior during its lifecycle. Angular Component Lifecycle Phases The lifecycle of a component mainly consists of three phases: 1. Creation Phase – Component is initialized. 2. Change Detection Phase – Angular checks and updates data. 3. Destruction Phase – Component is removed from the application. Important Angular Lifecycle Hooks Lifecycle Hook Description ngOnChanges() Called when input properties change. ngOnInit() Called once after the component is initialized. ngDoCheck() Called during every change detection cycle. ngAfterContentInit() Called after external content is projected into the component. ngAfterContentChecked() Called after projected content is checked. ngAfterViewInit() Called after the component’s view and child views are initialized. - Types of Modules in Angular
- In Angular, modules are used to organize the application into logical parts. Angular applications
- are divided into different modules to improve maintainability, scalability, and code reuse.
- There are several types of modules used in Angular development.
- 1. Root Module
- The Root Module is the main module that starts the Angular application. Every Angular
- application has at least one root module, usually called AppModule.
- It is responsible for bootstrapping the main component of the application.

Example @NgModule({ providers: [AuthService, LoggerService] }) export class CoreModule { } Services Q 13. What is an Angular Service? Explain why services are used in Angular applications. Solution Angular Service In Angular, a Service is a class used to perform specific tasks such as business logic, data sharing, and communication with external APIs. Services help keep the application organized by separating data handling and business logic from the user interface components. Services are commonly used together with Dependency Injection, which allows Angular to provide service instances to different components automatically. Purpose of Angular Services The main purpose of services is to provide reusable functionality that can be shared across multiple components in an Angular application. Instead of writing the same code in different components, developers create a service and reuse it wherever needed. Example of Angular Service student.service.ts import { Injectable } from '@angular/core' ; @Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }) export class StudentService { [DIAGRAM LABELS] - 4. Explain Dependency Injection in Angular with an example. Solution Dependency Injection in Angular In Angular, Dependency Injection (DI) is a design pattern used to provide required objects (dependencies) to a class instead of creating them inside the class. Angular has a built-in dependency injection system that automatically supplies services or objects to components and other classes. In simple terms, Dependency Injection allows Angular to create and manage service objects and inject them where they are needed. Why Dependency Injection is Used Dependency Injection is used in Angular to: ● Reduce tight coupling between classes ● Improve code reusability ● Make applications easier to test and maintain ● Share services across multiple components Instead of creating objects manually, Angular injects them automatically. Explain Data Binding in Angular. Describe its types with examples. Solution Data Binding in Angular is the mechanism that connects the data of the component (TypeScript code) with the view (HTML template). It allows automatic synchronization of data between the model (component) and the view (UI). In simple terms, data binding helps Angular display data in the UI and update it when the data changes. Why Data Binding is Important ● Reduces manual DOM manipulation. ● Automatically updates UI when data changes. ● Makes applications dynamic and interactive. ● Improves code readability and maintainability. Types of Data Binding in Angular Angular provides four main types of data binding. 1. Interpolation 2. Property Binding 3. Event Binding 4. What are Angular Directives? Explain different types of directives. Solution Angular Directives In Angular, Directives are special instructions in the DOM that extend the functionality of HTML elements. They allow developers to modify the appearance, behavior, or structure of elements in an Angular application. In simple terms, directives help Angular manipulate the DOM (Document Object Model) and create dynamic web applications. Example:

Welcome User

Here *ngIf is a directive that displays the paragraph only if the condition is true. Types of Angular Directives Angular directives are mainly divided into three types: 1. Component Directives 2. Structural Directives 3. Attribute Directives 1. Component Directives A Component Directive is a directive with a template (HTML view). It is the most common directive used in Angular. Solution Template-Driven Forms in Angular In Angular, Template-driven forms are a type of form where most of the form logic and validation are handled in the HTML template. Angular uses directives to create and manage forms directly in the template. Template-driven forms are simple and suitable for small applications or simple forms because they require less coding in TypeScript. They mainly rely on the FormsModule and the ngModel directive for two-way data binding. - Q 21. What is Angular Routing? Explain how navigation works in Angular applications.
- Solution
- Angular Routing
- In Angular, Routing is a mechanism that allows users to navigate between different views or
- components of an application without reloading the entire page. Angular routing is used to build
- Single Page Applications (SPAs) where only a part of the page changes when the user
- navigates.
- Routing helps developers map URLs to specific components, enabling smooth navigation
- between different pages such as Home, About, Contact, etc.
- Example URLs:
- /home
- /about
- /contact
- Each URL displays a different component.

Explain the process of configuring routes in Angular. Solution Configuring Routes in Angular In Angular, routing configuration is the process of defining how different URL paths map to specific components in an application. It allows users to navigate between views without reloading the page, which is a key feature of Single Page Applications (SPA). Routing is handled by the RouterModule, which manages navigation and displays components based on the URL. Steps to Configure Routes in Angular 1. Create Components First, create the components that will be used for navigation. Example components: ● HomeComponent ● AboutComponent ● ContactComponent Using Angular CLI: ng generate component home ng generate component about ng generate component contact 2. Import RouterModule Import RouterModule and define routes inside a routing module.

Import RouterModule Import RouterModule and define routes inside a routing module. Explain the HttpClient module in Angular and its role in API communication. Solution HttpClient Module in Angular In Angular, the HttpClient module is used to communicate with backend servers and APIs. It allows Angular applications to send HTTP requests and receive data from external sources such as databases or REST APIs. The HttpClient module is part of @angular/common/http and provides methods to perform operations such as: ● GET (retrieve data) ● POST (send data) ● PUT (update data) ● DELETE (remove data) These operations help Angular applications interact with backend technologies such as Node.js, Express, or databases. Role of HttpClient in API Communication The HttpClient module plays an important role in connecting the frontend Angular application with backend services. It is used to: ● Fetch data from APIs ● Send user input data to servers ● Update existing records ● Delete records from databases Example: retrieving user data from an API. - Advantages of Observables in Angular
- 1. Handle Asynchronous Operations
- Observables make it easier to work with asynchronous tasks such as HTTP requests and user
- interactions.
- 2. Multiple Values Over Time
- Observables can emit multiple values over time, unlike promises which return only one value.
- 3. Easy Data Stream Handling
- They help manage streams of data such as real-time updates, WebSocket data, or events.
- 4. Powerful Operators
- RxJS provides such
- many operators as:
- e map
- filter
- e merge
- debounce
- These help process data streams efficiently.

- adding, updating, and deleting data.

Describe how HTTP requests are used to communicate between Angular and Express.js. Solution HTTP Communication Between Angular and Express.js In a MEAN stack application, the frontend built using Angular communicates with the backend developed using Express.js running on Node.js. This communication is performed through HTTP requests, which allow Angular to send data to the server and receive responses from the backend API. Role of HTTP Requests HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is used as the communication protocol between the client (Angular) and server (Express.js). Angular sends requests such as: ● Fetch data ● Send form data ● Update records ● Delete records The Express.js server processes the request and returns a response, usually in JSON format. - Solution
- Role of RESTful APIs in Integrating Angular with Backend Services
- In modern web applications, the frontend built using Angular communicates with backend
- services using RESTful APIs. The backend is often developed using technologies such as
- Express.js running on Node.js and connected to databases like MongoDB.
- A RESTful API (Representational State Transfer API) is web service that allows different
- a
- systems to communicate with each other using HTTP requests and responses.
- RESTful APIs bridge between the Angular frontend and the backend
- act as a server.
- Role of RESTful APIs
- RESTful APIs enable Angular applications to send and receive data from backend systems.
- They allow the frontend to perform operations such as:
- e Fetching data from the server
- e Sending user input data
- e Updating existing records
- e Deleting data from the database
- This allows Angular applications become dynamic and data-driven.

Explain how Angular services are used to consume Express.js APIs. Solution Angular Services to Consume Express.js APIs In a full-stack web application, the frontend developed using Angular communicates with backend APIs created using Express.js running on Node.js. Angular services are used to send HTTP requests to the backend APIs and retrieve data. Services help keep API communication separate from the component logic, making the application more organized and maintainable. Role of Angular Services in API Communication Angular services are used to: ● Send HTTP requests to backend APIs ● Retrieve data from the server ● Send user data to the backend ● Handle business logic and data processing Instead of writing API calls in every component, developers create a single service that handles all API communication. Step 1: Create an Express.js API First, create an API in the backend server. Example: const express = require('express' ); const app = express(); app.get('/api/students' , (req, res) => { res.json([ { name: "Suraj", course: "CSE" }, { name: "Rahul", course: "IT" } ]); }); app.listen(3000); This API returns student data in JSON format. - Importance of Authentication in Angular Applications
- Authentication plays a critical role in Angular applications for several
- reasons.
- 1. Security
- Authentication ensures that only verified users can access the application, preventing
- unauthorized access.
- 2. Access Control
- It allows developers to restrict access to certain pages or features based on user identity.
- Example:
- Admin dashboard
- e
- e User profile page
- 3. Route Protection
- Angular applications can protect routes using route guards to ensure that only authenticated
- users can navigate to certain pages.
- /login — accessible to everyone
- /dashboard — accessible only to logged- users
- 4. Data Protection
- Authentication ensures that sensitive user data is only available to authorized users.
- Banking information
- e Personal user details

- Solution
- Authorization and Role-Based Access Control in Angular Applications
- In web applications, authorization is the of determining what actions a is allowed to
- process user
- perform after authentication. While authentication verifies the identity of a user, authorization
- ensures that the user can only access resources that they are permitted to use.
- In applications built with Angular, authorization is commonly implemented using Role-Based
- Access Control (RBAC). In RBAC, users are assigned roles such as Admin, Manager, or User,
- and access to different parts of the application is controlled based on these roles.
- Concept of Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
- Role-Based Access Control works by assigning permissions according to user roles.
- Example:
- Role Access Permission
- Admin Full access to system
- Manager Access to reports and management tools
- User Access to personal profile and limited features
- Implementation Steps in Angular
- 1. Authenticate the User
- First, the user logs in through the Angular application. The backend server verifies the
- credentials and returns a token containing user information and role.

- " admin"
- Angular stores this token in localStorage or sessionStorage.

- After login, Angular extracts the role from the authentication
- response.
- localStorage etItem( » respons ole);
- The role will be used to control access to routes and features.

- Q 13. What are WebSockets? Explain how they are used for real-time communication in
- web applications.
- Solution
- WebSockets
- WebSockets are a communication protocol that enables real-time, two-way (bidirectional)
- communication between a client and a server over a single, persistent connection. Unlike
- traditional HTTP requests, WebSockets allow data to be sent and received instantly without
- repeatedly opening new connections.
- WebSockets are widely used in modern applications built with frameworks such as Angular on
- the frontend and servers like Node.js or Express.js on the backend.
